support_note: Pure M/M/1 is the canonical form used to teach basic queueing behavior — the wait-time-vs-utilization curve is most cleanly visible in the canonical model without distractions from variants.
Pure M/M/1 is the canonical validation benchmark — its closed-form L = rho/(1-rho), W = 1/(mu-lambda) are the reference values that simulation output must match.

Pure M/M/1 serves Scenario 3 as the baseline against which a lower-variance comparison is made. Fully demonstrating the Pollaczek-Khinchine insight requires M/G/1 which is a separate blueprint (future gap-driven walkthrough).
Pure M/M/1 is an ideal setting for demonstrating Little's Law — its closed-form L and W make the identity easy to verify.
M/M/1/K shows a variant of Scenario 1 where wait time saturates because the queue is bounded — arrivals beyond capacity are lost rather than accumulating.
Little's Law holds for any stable queueing system including M/M/1/K (adjusted to account for effective arrival rate).
Reneging makes Scenario 1 more complex — as rho approaches 1, observed wait times are bounded by the patience distribution rather than growing without bound.
Little's Law applies to the reneging variant with effective arrival and departure rates.
Balking modifies Scenario 1 by reducing effective arrival rate as the queue grows, creating a self-limiting feedback effect.
Little's Law applies to the balking variant with the effective (post-balking) arrival rate.
